The other major source of conflict besides artifacts either internally within themselves or between each other is the assignment directions.

Assignment directions are often created in wildly different ways across the organization, and that means that whatever instructions you're giving there, which are a prompt, by the way, like what is assignment directions?
What is that field?
It's a prompt.
It's a So...
Remember that when you're writing this, it's not some sort of, it's not some sort of like detailed fill in the blanks or Mad Libs.
It is a prompt, which means that it will be interpreted as a prompt by Sonnet, which is what it is called to interpret that prompt by the workflow.
So when you're writing assignment directions, just as you would with the prompt, you want to do two major things.

One, check for conflicts between that and your contextual artifacts, because if you're telling it to do something in assignment directions that is oppositional to what writing and guidelines is saying, then it's going to run into issues the moment that the drafter calls both the assignment directions and the writing guidelines and tries to rectify, right?
So that is also part of your conflict pass.
The second thing that's really important here is that assignment directions really are directional.
They are not hyper explicit.
So
You do not need to, nor should you, create an enormously complicated, detailed, full brief and paste it into assignment directions.
That is the wrong way to go.
You are, like, paddling up the wrong creek very, very fast.
It is not impossible that you will get something workable out of it, but I guarantee you that your end processing times will likely be higher.
And by that, I mean, once it exits the pipeline, how long does it take you to get a client deliverable, which we'll talk about in minute.
But I guarantee you those times will be higher if you give it a laborious amount of information in that field.
Because it's a prompt.
You can't write a 2,500-word prompt and expect it to adhere to every single item in that.
It's just not viable.
Not only that, but if you give it an incredibly detailed outline, especially, like many of you do, which is fine.

Like what gymnastics would they have to pull to do like seven or eight different major thought exercises in 150 words?

It usually results in compound sentences, wild constructions, colons, because colons are great for that, M dashes, of course, because if you need a sentence to encompass two or three different thoughts, are you going to do?
Throw an M dash in it, right?

If you have specific formats that you needed to follow, it can be useful to have a separate workflow that adjusts the format of a section or inserts a section with hyper-specific formatting on its own so that you have the space to do that task holistically on its own, like, hey, just please do this.
It can be useful to isolate issues and to help you create a better outcome without having to be like, oh, no, how do I adjust this 2,000-word document to, like, produce this desired outcome?
I think one instance of it is we had a client that has a CTA that they wanted to be narratively integrated.
So instead of, like, hey, insert this CTA every time, which is fairly easy to do in writing guidelines, you can say, like, this is the CTA you use.
Just don't use a different CTA.
